name: Kuwaiti Arabic

ancestors.notes: Gulf Arabic base — inherits *q→[ɡ], velar affrication, interdental retention and emphatic spreading.

notes: Kuwaiti Arabic — the best-documented Gulf variety. Inherits the Gulf features: *q → [ɡ] (with [q] a formal/MSA candidate); velar affrication /k/→[tʃ] and /ɡ/→[dʒ] before high front /i, iː/; interdentals /θ ð ðˤ/ retained. Kuwaiti-specific: Matar (1969), as reported in Alshammari 2026 p.1335, describes /ɡ/ → [dʒ] adjacent to front vowels, sometimes across an intervening /r, l/ (e.g. qariːb→dʒiriːb), and in emphatic contexts (ðˤiːg → ðˤiːdʒ) — the non-adjacent trigger is NOT modelled (see GULF_G_AFFRICATION notes). The 2nd-person-feminine-singular suffix affricates to -tʃ (kashkasha), a morphological process outside base-orthography scope. Heavy English influence (post-1950s oil era): /p/, /v/, /ŋ/ well-established from loanwords. Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic.

sources.author: Alshammari, K. K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: Read in full (open-access PDF). Reports (p.1335) Matar's (1969) study of Kuwaiti /ɡ/→[dʒ] affrication adjacent to front vowels and in emphatic context, and Johnstone (1967) on the lexicalisation of velar affrication in Eastern Arabic.

sources.author: Al-Balushi, R.
sources.title: Omani Arabic: More than a Dialect
sources.notes: Read the phonology sections. Notes /q/→[ɣ] in some Kuwaiti varieties (e.g. ɣirš 'shark' < qirš), cross-referenced with Eastern Arabian /q/ reflexes (p.89).

location.notes: Kuwait City. A national variety within the Gulf continuum. [Wikidata Q35178 P625]

stress.notes: Arabic stress is QUANTITY-SENSITIVE: it falls on a syllable because that syllable is heavy, not because of any orthographic ending, so no ending table can express it. The cascade (Ryding, A Reference Grammar of MSA, CUP 2005, §2.3; Watson, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP 2002, ch.3): a superheavy final syllable (CVːC/CVCC) takes the stress (kiˈtaːb); otherwise a heavy penult (CVː/CVC) does (muˈdarris); otherwise the antepenult (ˈmadrasa). max_onset=1 because Arabic onsets are obligatory and hold exactly one consonant — a medial cluster splits, closing the preceding syllable, and that boundary is what makes the penult of mudarris heavy. INPUT CONTRACT: weight is only recoverable from a complete transcription, so this requires diacritized input; an undiacritized skeleton has no vowels to weigh. The registered wikipron gold carries no stress marks, but the benchmark strips stress from both sides by default, so this costs no PER. Prosodic clitics: monosyllabic function words (negator maː, prepositions fiː/min/ʕan/ʕa/li/bi, vocative jaː, copula-negator muː/miʃ, conditional law/loː, wa-proclitic combinations) attach to a host and fall inside the host's stress domain, so they carry no word stress (Watson 2002, The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic, OUP, ch.3). Pan-Arabic class; the per-lect form list reflects the function words attested in this lect. Orthographic test — a clitic homograph (e.g. verb yā 'came') is destressed in every occurrence.
